options.text: A real contact force between two objects that obeys Newton's Third Law
A fictitious force added in a non-inertial frame to make F = ma work
A gravitational force due to the mass of the Earth acting on a body
An electromagnetic force between charged particles in a magnetic field

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. An object at rest stays at rest, and a moving object keeps moving, unless acted on by a net force (Newton's First Law). The answer is 'A fictitious force added in a non-inertial frame to make F = ma work'. The other options — 'A real contact force between two objects that obeys Newton's Third Law', 'A gravitational force due to the mass of the Earth acting on a body', and 'An electromagnetic force between charged particles in a magnetic field' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is centrifugal force a real force?

options.text: Yes — it is a fundamental force
Yes — it acts in all frames
No — it is a fictitious force in a rotating frame
Yes — it is a contact force

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal force — An outward pseudo-force felt in a rotating frame. It's not a real force — it's the result of inertia in a rotating reference frame. The other options — 'Yes — it is a fundamental force', 'Yes — it is a contact force', and 'Yes — it acts in all frames' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which direction does the Coriolis force deflect objects in the Northern Hemisphere?
quiz_questions.explanation: It deflects winds on Earth (right in the Northern Hemisphere, left in the Southern). Coriolis force — Affects moving objects in a rotating frame. The other options — 'To the left', 'Downward', and 'Upward' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

Key Point Pseudo-forces are mathematical tools — they have no real physical source (no action-reaction pair). Key Concepts — Non-Inertial Frames & Pseudo-Forces Non-Inertial Reference Frames A non-inertial frame is one that is accelerating. The other options — 'Yes, always', 'Only in rotating frames', and 'Only at high speeds' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do you feel pushed backward when a car accelerates forward?

options.text: Pseudo-force in the non-inertial frame of the car

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. An object at rest stays at rest, and a moving object keeps moving, unless acted on by a net force (Newton's First Law). The answer is 'Pseudo-force in the non-inertial frame of the car'. The other options — 'The seat pushes you back', 'Gravity increases', and 'Air resistance pushes you' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a non-inertial reference frame?

options.text: Any frame moving at constant velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Non-Inertial Frames & Pseudo-Forces Non-Inertial Reference Frames A non-inertial frame is one that is accelerating. Common Pseudo-Forces Pseudo-force in a linearly accelerating frame : F_pseudo = −ma_frame. The other options — 'Any frame at rest', 'Any frame moving at constant velocity', and 'A frame attached to Earth' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What must you add to apply Newton's laws in an accelerating frame?
quiz_questions.explanation: To use F = ma in a non-inertial frame, we introduce pseudo-forces (fictitious forces). Key Concepts — Non-Inertial Frames & Pseudo-Forces Non-Inertial Reference Frames A non-inertial frame is one that is accelerating. The other options — 'Real forces', 'More mass', and 'Extra dimensions' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.
